docs.title: N.O.M.A.D. Field Desk
docs.text: N.O.M.A.D. Field Desk is an offline-first preparedness and field-operations desktop app. Everything — AI chat, encyclopedias, maps, medical records, supply tracking — runs fully offline after initial setup.

docs.title: Features at a Glance
docs.text:
• Fully offline after setup — no cloud, no subscriptions, no telemetry
  • AI Chat powered by local LLMs (Llama, Mistral, Phi) via llama.cpp
  • Information Library — offline Wikipedia, medical references, survival guides
  • Offline Maps — downloadable tile packs for your region
  • 25-tab Preparedness workspace — inventory, medical, power, comms, security, and more
  • Interactive Decision Guides — 13 step-by-step guides with 300+ decision nodes
  • LAN multi-device — family/team members connect via local network browser

docs.title: Quick Links
docs.text:
• Getting Started
  • Preparedness (25 Sub-Tabs)
  • AI Assistant Guide
  • Day One Checklist
  • FAQ
  • Glossary

docs.title: Download
docs.text: Latest release: GitHub Releases

docs.title: get_db() leak audit — factory-loop iteration 2
docs.text:
Total suspect call sites: 5 across 2 files.

Generated by tools/audit_db_sessions.py. A finding here means a get_db() call isn't paired with either a db_session() context manager (preferred) or a try/finally: db.close() pair. Each is a candidate pooled-connection leak.

docs.title: Severity legend
docs.text:
• assigned-but-not-closed — db = get_db() without a try/finally that closes db. Highest priority; the worker holds a live connection for the caller's lifetime.
  • bare-call — get_db().execute(...) as an expression. The connection is abandoned after the chained call; the pool will eventually reclaim it but transactions may linger.

docs.title: Known-safe patterns (not leaks)
docs.text:
These shapes still appear in the table because the classifier is deliberately precise, but they are intentional:

  • db.py::_pool_acquire returning get_db() — the pool function's contract is to hand ownership of a fresh connection to its caller, which then returns it via the db_session() context manager on exit. The pool itself can't close what it's yielding.
  • tests/test_db_safety.py::test_get_db_* — tests that exercise get_db()'s OWN cleanup-on-failure behaviour. Skipping close in the test body is the scenario under test.
  • tests/conftest.py::db fixture — a "keeper" connection that persists for the shared in-memory SQLite URI throughout the fixture's lifetime, then closes on teardown via yield semantics.

docs.title: Top-level summary
docs.text: Top-level Findings db.py/ 3 tests/ 2

docs.title: db.py (3 findings)
docs.text: Location Function Severity Snippet db.py:99 _pool_acquire bare-call return get_db(), False db.py:109 _pool_acquire bare-call return get_db(), False db.py:125 _pool_acquire bare-call return get_db(), False

docs.title: tests/test_db_safety.py (2 findings)
docs.text: Location Function Severity Snippet tests/test_db_safety.py:60 test_get_db_closes_connection_if_setup_fails bare-call db.get_db() tests/test_db_safety.py:82 test_get_db_keeps_connection_open_if_flask_context_binding_fails assigned-but-not-closed conn = db.get_db()

docs.title: Shared Offline Basemap
docs.text: NukeMap and VIPTrack now share one richer offline basemap instead of each using a separate lightweight cache.

docs.title: What Changed
docs.text:
• Both workspaces use the same local storage cache key: nomad-offline-atlas-cache
  • Both workspaces install or refresh the basemap when they launch/onboard if the cache is missing or outdated
  • The bundled source atlas lives at web/nukemap/data/offline_atlas.json
  • The atlas is generated by tools/build_offline_atlas.py

docs.title: Basemap Contents
docs.text:
The generated atlas currently includes Natural Earth 1:50m data for:

  • land polygons
  • coastlines
  • country borders
  • admin-1 borders (state/province outlines)
  • lakes
  • rivers
  • populated places

docs.title: Runtime Flow
docs.text:
1. NukeMap or VIPTrack launches.
  2. The workspace checks nomad-offline-atlas-cache.
  3. If the cache is missing or the atlas version changed, it fetches /nukemap/data/offline_atlas.json.
  4. The downloaded atlas is cached once and reused by both workspaces.
  5. Light and dark themes restyle the same atlas so both tools stay visually aligned with NOMAD themes.

docs.title: Files Touched
docs.text:
• tools/build_offline_atlas.py
  • web/nukemap/data/offline_atlas.json
  • web/nukemap/js/app.js
  • web/viptrack/index.html
  • web/templates/index_partials/_tab_nukemap.html
  • web/nukemap/index.html

docs.title: Reset / Reinstall
docs.text:
If you need to force a clean reinstall of the shared basemap:

  • remove localStorage['nomad-offline-atlas-cache']
  • optionally clear onboarding flags such as nukemap-welcomed and viptrack_onboarded
  • relaunch NukeMap or VIPTrack so the basemap is downloaded again

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
The current implementation was verified with:

  • python tools/build_offline_atlas.py
  • npm run build
  • python -m pytest -q tests/test_core.py
  • npx playwright test tests/ui/shell-workflows.spec.mjs --grep "nukemap fills the wide workspace frame and follows the active shell theme|viptrack fills the wide workspace frame and keeps embedded controls stateful"
  • npm run visual:test
  • python -m pytest -q

docs.title: 1. Getting Started
docs.text: When you first open NOMAD, the Setup Wizard walks you through picking a storage drive and choosing what to download. You can re-run it anytime from Settings > Re-run Setup Wizard.

docs.title: Recommended First Steps
docs.text:
• Install AI Chat — Click "Install AI Chat" on the Home page. This downloads the AI engine (~310 MB). Takes 2-5 minutes.

  • Download an AI Model — Go to AI Assistant in the sidebar, click "+ Download AI Model." Pick a small model like Llama 3.2 3B (2 GB) to start. Larger models give better answers but need more disk space and RAM.

  • Install the Information Library — Click "Install Encyclopedia" on the Home page. Then go to the Library tab and click Download All Essentials to get offline Wikipedia, medical references, and survival guides.

  • Start Using Preparedness Tools — Click Preparedness in the sidebar. Start by adding your family members to Contacts, then add your supplies to Inventory.

  • Try the Interactive Guides — Go to Preparedness > Guides. These step-by-step decision trees help you through real situations (water purification, wound care, fire starting, etc.) — no AI needed.

Tip: The entire setup takes about 10-15 minutes on a decent internet connection. After that, everything works offline forever.

docs.title: 2. Home Dashboard
docs.text:
The Home page is your command center overview. It shows: - Status indicator — Overall threat level based on your situation board (green = all clear, yellow = caution, red = critical) - Low stock & expiring items — Click these to jump directly to your inventory - Incidents (24h) — Recent security or safety events you have logged - Readiness Score — Your overall preparedness grade (see below) - Feature cards — Quick access to each major section - Service status — Which tools are installed and running - Activity feed — Recent events (services started, items added, alerts triggered)

Tip: Use the search bar at the top to find anything — supplies, contacts, notes, checklists, conversations, and documents.

docs.title: 3. Readiness Score
docs.text:
The Readiness Score is a letter grade (A through F) that measures how prepared you are across 7 categories:

Category Max Points What Improves It Water 20 Adding water supplies to your inventory Food 20 Adding food items, tracking daily usage, removing expired items Medical 15 Adding medical supplies, registering patients, recording blood types Security 10 Adding cameras, logging access events, resolving incidents Communications 10 Adding contacts, logging radio communications Power & Land 10 Registering power devices, adding garden plots, saving map waypoints Plans & Knowledge 15 Completing checklists, writing notes, uploading documents, running drills

Click any category to jump directly to the relevant Preparedness section where you can improve that score.

docs.title: 4. AI Assistant
docs.text: The AI runs entirely on your computer — nothing is sent to the internet. It can answer questions about survival, medical care, homesteading, radio communications, and more.

docs.title: How to Use It
docs.text:
1. Make sure the AI Chat service is running (green status on Home page)
  2. Select an AI model from the dropdown (top-left of the chat area)
  3. Choose a conversation mode from the dropdown — this tells the AI what kind of expert to be (Medical Advisor, Survival Expert, Homesteading, etc.)
  4. Type your question and press Enter or click Send

docs.title: Include My Prep Data
docs.text: Toggle "Include My Prep Data" to let the AI see your inventory levels, contacts, weather logs, power status, and alerts. This means it can give advice based on YOUR actual situation rather than generic answers. Your data stays on your computer.

docs.title: Document Intelligence
docs.text:
Upload PDFs, text files, or other documents in the Library tab. The AI can: - Automatically classify documents (medical, property, financial, etc.) - Generate a 2-3 sentence summary - Extract key information (names, dates, medications, addresses) - Cross-reference extracted names against your contacts

Tip: The AI models are downloaded once and work forever offline. Larger models (like Mistral 7B or Llama 3.1 8B) give much better answers than small ones. If you have a GPU, the AI will automatically use it for faster responses.

docs.title: 5. Information Library
docs.text:
The Library contains 100+ downloadable content packs organized into 14 categories. Content is available in 3 tiers: - Essential — Core references: Wikipedia, emergency guides, survival skills (~2-5 GB) - Standard — Adds medical databases, repair guides, cooking, agriculture - Everything — Complete collection including history, science, fiction, technical docs

Use the Download All Essentials button to get started quickly. You can also upload your own PDFs and documents for offline reading.

docs.title: 6. Offline Maps
docs.text: Download maps for your region so you can navigate without cell service or internet. Select from 22 worldwide regions.

docs.title: 10 Map Tools
docs.text:
Tool What It Does Drop Pin Mark a point on the map with a label Measure Measure distance between two points Save Waypoint Save a named location to your database Draw Zone Draw a colored area on the map Property Boundary Draw a polygon and calculate area (acres) and perimeter (feet/miles) Clear Pins Remove all temporary markers Print Layout Generate a printable map page with title, compass, and scale Bookmark Save and recall specific map views Bearing & Distance Calculate direction (degrees + cardinal) and distance between two points Export GPX Export all saved waypoints to a GPS-compatible file

Tip: Enter coordinates directly (like "38.8977, -77.0365") in the search box to jump to a specific location.

docs.title: 7. Notes
docs.text: Write and organize notes, plans, SOPs, and logs. Notes auto-save as you type. Features include: - Tags — Add comma-separated tags to organize notes - Pinning — Pin important notes to the top of the list - Live preview — See formatted output as you type - Export — Export individual notes as .md files or all notes as a ZIP

docs.title: NukeMap v3.2.0
docs.text: Nuclear effects simulator with 418 real-world targets, 708 warheads, and 7 conflict scenarios. Visualize blast radius, thermal radiation, fallout patterns, and shelter requirements for any location.

docs.title: Training Drills
docs.text: 6 timed drill scenarios with step-by-step checklists: 72-Hour Kit Check, Evacuation Drill, Blackout Drill, Communication Test, Shelter-in-Place, and First Aid. Results are saved to your drill history.

docs.title: Off-Grid Radio Messaging
docs.text: Connect a Meshtastic radio device via USB to send and receive text messages without cell towers or internet. Range: 1-10+ miles depending on terrain. Buy a Meshtastic radio, plug it in, and click "Scan for Devices."

docs.title: Barcode Scanner
docs.text: Use your camera to scan barcodes and quickly add items to your inventory. Requires camera permission.

docs.title: Video Library
docs.text: Upload and organize instructional videos (survival, medical, repair, cooking, etc.) for offline viewing.

docs.title: 9. Preparedness (25 Sub-Tabs)
docs.text:
The Preparedness section is the heart of NOMAD. It contains 25 sub-tabs ordered by emergency priority:

Tab What It Does Key Features Inventory Track all your supplies Quantities, expiration dates, daily usage tracking, "Days Left" projections, low-stock alerts, shopping list generator, CSV import/export, quick +/- buttons, one-click Daily Consume Contacts Emergency people directory Names, callsigns, roles, skills, blood types, rally points, medical notes. Skills matrix shows coverage gaps. CSV import/export. Quick-add 7 standard emergency numbers. Checklists Readiness checklists 15 built-in templates (72hr kit, bug-out bag, vehicle kit, winter storm, CBRN shelter, infant kit, etc.). Create custom checklists. JSON import/export for sharing. Medical Health tracking Patient profiles linked to contacts. Vital signs (BP, pulse, respiration, temperature, SpO2, pain 0-10, GCS 3-15) with color-coded abnormals. Wound documentation (8 types, 4 severities). 26-pair drug interaction checker. Printable patient care cards. Import patients from contacts with one click. Incidents Event log Record security, medical, infrastructure, weather, and supply events with severity levels and category tags. Filter and search history. Family Plan Emergency family plan FEMA-style plan: 3 meeting locations, 3 evacuation routes, household member cards (medical info, blood types), insurance and utility account numbers. Auto-saves as you type. Print emergency card. Security Physical security Camera viewer (MJPEG live feeds, snapshot auto-refresh, HLS streams). Access log (entry/exit/patrol tracking). Security dashboard with threat level, camera count, and incident summary. Power Energy management Device registry for solar panels, batteries, charge controllers, inverters, and generators with specs. Power log (voltage, SOC%, solar/load watts). Autonomy projection dashboard with color-coded gauges (green >7d, orange >3d, red <3d). Garden Food production Garden plots with dimensions and area calculation. Seed inventory with automatic viability tracking (25 species). Harvest log that automatically adds produce to your inventory. Livestock records (10 species) with health event logging. USDA hardiness zone lookup by latitude. Weather Weather tracking Manual weather observations: pressure, temperature, wind, clouds, visibility. Barometric pressure trend analysis for storm prediction. Pressure drops >4 hPa trigger alerts. Guides Step-by-step decision trees 13 interactive guides with 300+ decision nodes: water purification, wound assessment, fire starting, shelter construction, radio setup, food preservation, START triage, power outage response, vehicle emergency, bug-out vs shelter decision, antibiotic selection (symptom-to-treatment), water source assessment (source-to-purification method), and food safety assessment (is this safe to eat?). "Ask AI" button at any step. Works fully offline. Calculators Survival math Water storage, food storage (LDS/FEMA), generator fuel, rainwater harvest, radio range (12 radio types), medication dosage, solar panel sizing, bug-out bag weight, resource planning, travel time, battery life, and bleach dosing calculators. Procedures Emergency procedures 17 step-by-step protocols: CPR, bleeding control, water purification, shelter building, fire starting, choking, hypothermia, wound closure, burns, fractures, snake bite, anaphylaxis, dental emergency, psychological first aid, CERT, and natural disaster procedures. Search and expand-all. Printable wallet card. Radio Frequency reference Complete radio frequency table: NOAA weather (7 channels), FRS (22 channels), GMRS, MURS, CB (40 channels), HAM bands (2m, 70cm, HF), and shortwave stations. Quick Ref Reference cards 30+ quick reference topics: NATO phonetic alphabet, Morse code with audio trainer, unit converter, triage protocols, companion planting chart, calorie database, livestock care, WHO essential medicines, knots, navigation, sanitation, wild edibles, CBRN decontamination guide, water purification method comparison, antibiotic field reference, PACE communications planning, EMP/CME hardening guide, wound care & closure field guide, agricultural calorie & storage guide, and vehicle bug-out preparedness. Signals Emergency signals Ground-to-air visual signals, sound signal patterns, smoke signal guide for rescue communication. Command Post Tactical operations Situation Report (SITREP) generator with military format. Message cipher (Caesar, Reverse, Atbash). Infrastructure status tracker (12 utilities). Vehicle readiness board. Threat assessment matrix. After-event review form. Emergency broadcast to all LAN devices. 35-item home security hardening assessment with scoring. Journal Daily log Daily journal entries with mood tracking (5 moods), tag system, chronological timeline, and full export. Secure Vault Encrypted storage AES-256-GCM encrypted entries for sensitive information (passwords, coordinates, account numbers, legal documents). Master password required to unlock. Password generator with strength indicator. Legal document templates. Skills Survival skill tracking Self-assessment for 60 survival skills across 10 categories: Fire, Water, Shelter, Food, Navigation, Medical, Communications, Security, Mechanical, Homesteading. Proficiency levels: None, Basic, Intermediate, Expert. Category summary dashboard showing coverage. Seed-defaults button to load all 60 skills at once. Skill proficiency counts toward your Readiness Score. Ammo Ammunition inventory Track ammunition by caliber, brand, bullet weight, bullet type, quantity, and storage location. Caliber-grouped summary cards showing total rounds per caliber. Full CRUD table with search. Ammo reserve contributes to your Security readiness score. Community Mutual aid network Registry of community members, neighbors, and local resources for mutual aid planning. Track name, distance, skills, equipment, contact info, and trust level (Unknown / Acquaintance / Trusted / Inner-Circle). Trusted community members improve your Planning & Knowledge score. Radiation Radiation dose tracking Log radiation dose rate readings (R/hr or rem/hr) with cumulative dose calculation. Dashboard shows total accumulated dose, last reading, and time since first reading. Reference table of dose effects. 7-10 Rule explanation for fallout decay. Uses the nuclear 7-10 Rule: fallout drops 90% for every 7x time elapsed since detonation. Fuel Fuel storage management Track stored gasoline, diesel, propane, kerosene, and other fuels by type, quantity, container, storage location, and expiration date. Stabilizer tracking (extends gasoline shelf life to 2 years). Shelf life reference table. Fuel reserves contribute to your Shelter & Power readiness score. Color-coded expiration warnings. Equipment Maintenance log Track critical equipment: generators, vehicles, power tools, communications gear, medical devices, and water filtration. Log last service date, next service due, service notes, and operational status. One-click "Mark Serviced" button. Status dashboard shows operational vs. needs-service counts. Color-coded overdue service warnings.

docs.title: 10. Proactive Alerts
docs.text:
A background engine automatically checks every 5 minutes for conditions that need your attention: - Supply consumption warnings — Items with less than 7 days supply remaining - Expiring items — Supplies expiring within the next 14 days - Barometric pressure drops — Pressure falling more than 4 hPa (storm indicator) - Incident clusters — 3 or more incidents logged within 48 hours - Low stock — Items that have fallen below their minimum quantity

Alerts appear as a badge on the bell icon in the sidebar. Click to see details. Click AI Summary for a natural-language situation report. Critical alerts trigger browser notifications and an alert sound.

Tip: Set daily usage rates on your inventory items (the "Daily Usage" field) to get accurate "Days Left" projections and timely low-supply alerts.

docs.title: 11. Connecting Multiple Systems
docs.text: If you have NOMAD running on multiple computers (e.g., your home PC and a cabin laptop), you can sync data between them:

docs.title: Network Sync (same network)
docs.text:
1. Go to Settings > Connect Multiple Systems
  2. Give this system a name (e.g., "Base Camp")
  3. Click Scan for Nodes to find other NOMAD systems on your network
  4. Click Push Data to send your inventory, contacts, checklists, notes, incidents, and waypoints
  5. Or click Pull Data to receive data from another system

docs.title: USB / Offline Transfer (no network needed)
docs.text: In Settings, use USB / Offline Transfer to export a portable ZIP file. Copy it to a USB drive, carry it to the other system, and import it there. Data is always merged — nothing gets overwritten.

docs.title: 12. Training Scenarios
docs.text:
Practice your emergency response with 4 realistic multi-phase simulations in the Tools tab:

Scenario Phases Description Grid Down — 7 Days 7 Progressive power failure: water loss, food decisions, security, medical, communications, recovery Medical Crisis 5 Trauma response: assessment, bleeding control, pain management, monitoring, complications Evacuation Under Threat 5 Emergency departure: warning, packing, route decisions, roadblocks, arrival Winter Storm Survival 5 Cold weather: heating crisis, fuel rationing, pipe burst, neighbor aid, rescue

If the AI is running, it generates realistic complications between phases based on your actual inventory and contacts data. Each run is scored 0-100 with an after-event review that identifies what you did well and what to improve.

docs.title: 13. Themes
docs.text: Five themes available (use the quick theme controls in the sidebar footer or the full settings workspace): - Atlas — Warm daylight workspace with soft surfaces and calm contrast - Midnight — Deep neutral dark theme for long operating sessions - Cobalt — Blue-steel dark theme with cooler accents for analysis-heavy work - Ember — Warm dark theme with stronger urgency and night-operations character - Paper — Quiet low-distraction mode for reading, printing, and e-ink style use

docs.title: 14. Settings & Backup
docs.text:
• System Monitoring — Live CPU, RAM, and disk usage gauges
  • AI Models — Manage downloaded models. Click "Download All Recommended" to get all suggested models.
  • Preferences — Theme, AI assistant name, external AI host, dashboard password, auto-backup interval, browser notifications
  • Full Backup / Restore — Download a ZIP of your entire database, or restore from a previous backup
  • Data Summary — See how many records you have across all tables
  • Application Log — View and filter system events for troubleshooting
  • Start with Windows — Toggle to have NOMAD launch automatically at startup

Important: Back up regularly! Use Settings > Full Backup to download a ZIP file. Store copies on USB drives in multiple locations. The "Last Backup" indicator turns orange after 30 days and red after that.

docs.title: 15. Diagnostics
docs.text: Test your system's CPU, memory, disk, and AI performance. Results are scored 0-100 (NOMAD Score). Run it periodically to confirm your hardware is performing well. Trend arrows show whether performance improved or declined since the last run.

docs.title: 16. Keyboard Shortcuts
docs.text: Shortcut Action Alt+1 through Alt+9 Switch between the primary workspaces Alt+T Open/close the timer widget Alt+C Open/close LAN chat Alt+N Create a new note Ctrl+K Open the command palette Escape Close any open overlay, panel, or dialog

docs.title: 17. Data & Privacy
docs.text:
All data stays on your computer. NOMAD has zero telemetry, zero cloud connections, and zero tracking. The only time it connects to the internet is when YOU choose to download services, content packs, or AI models.

Data is stored in %APPDATA%\\\\NOMADFieldDesk\\\\ on new installs (or the custom location you chose during setup). Upgraded systems may still use the legacy %APPDATA%\\\\ProjectNOMAD\\\\ folder. This includes: - nomad.db — SQLite database with all your data (32 tables) - logs/ — Application logs - backups/ — Automatic database backups (keeps last 5) - services/ — Downloaded service binaries and AI models - maps/ — Downloaded offline map data - videos/ — Your uploaded video library - kb_uploads/ — Uploaded documents for AI analysis

docs.title: AI Chat says "Could not reach the AI"
docs.text: The AI Chat service needs to be running. Go to the Home page and check if AI Chat shows a green "Running" badge. If not, click Start. If it is not installed yet, click Install first (~310 MB download).

docs.title: Service won't start
docs.text: Check the Application Log in Settings for error details. Common causes: another program is using the same port, antivirus is blocking the executable, or the service files are corrupted (try uninstalling and reinstalling).

docs.title: Map downloads fail with "permission denied"
docs.text: Your antivirus may be blocking the map download tool. Try adding the NOMAD data folder to your antivirus exclusions, or run as Administrator.

docs.title: Content packs are stuck downloading
docs.text: Check your internet connection. Downloads resume where they left off if interrupted. If a download is truly stuck, restart the application.

docs.title: Everything appears frozen / buttons do nothing
docs.text: Try pressing Ctrl+Shift+R to force-reload the page. If running from the portable exe, make sure you are running the latest version from the releases page.

docs.title: LAN access from other devices
docs.text: Other devices on your network can access NOMAD by opening a browser and going to http://YOUR_IP:8080. Your LAN address is shown in the Settings tab. Make sure your firewall allows port 8080.

docs.title: Need more help?
docs.text: [Visit the GitHub Issues](https://github.com/SysAdminDoc/project-nomad-desktop/issues) page or join the Crosstalk Solutions Discord community.

docs.title: 19. Day One Checklist
docs.text:
If you just installed NOMAD and want to be operational as fast as possible, follow this checklist:

Priority Task Time 1 Run the Setup Wizard — choose your storage drive and Essential content 2 min 2 Install AI Chat from the Home page 3 min 3 Download a small AI model (Llama 3.2 3B or Phi 3 Mini) 5 min 4 Add your household members to Contacts (name, phone, blood type) 5 min 5 Add your critical supplies to Inventory (water, food, medical, batteries) 15 min 6 Set daily usage rates on consumable items (water, food staples) 5 min 7 Fill out the Family Emergency Plan (meeting points, evacuation routes) 10 min 8 Download your regional map for offline navigation 5 min 9 Complete at least one built-in Checklist (72-Hour Kit recommended) 10 min 10 Run through one Interactive Guide (Water Purification is a good start) 5 min

Result: After completing these 10 steps (~65 minutes), you will have a functional offline command center with AI chat, offline maps, supply tracking with automated alerts, and a family emergency plan. Your Readiness Score should jump to at least a C or B grade.

docs.title: 20. Choosing the Right AI Model
docs.text:
AI models vary in size, speed, and quality. Here is a guide to help you choose:

Model Size RAM Needed Best For Speed Phi 3 Mini 2.3 GB 4 GB Quick answers, older PCs, limited RAM Fast Llama 3.2 3B 2.0 GB 4 GB General questions, good balance of speed and quality Fast Gemma 3 4B 3.3 GB 6 GB Better reasoning, multilingual support Medium Mistral 7B 4.1 GB 8 GB High-quality answers, good for medical and technical questions Medium Llama 3.1 8B 4.7 GB 8 GB Best overall quality for 8GB systems Medium Qwen3 8B 4.9 GB 8 GB Latest generation, strong reasoning and coding Medium MedGemma 5.0 GB 8 GB Medical-specific: symptoms, medications, conditions Medium DeepSeek-R1 14B 9.0 GB 16 GB Complex reasoning, detailed analysis, planning Slower

Recommendation: Start with Llama 3.2 3B (works on any PC). If you have 8+ GB RAM, upgrade to Mistral 7B for much better answers. If you have a GPU (NVIDIA/AMD), models run 5-10x faster.

Note: Models are downloaded once and stored permanently. You can have multiple models installed and switch between them in the AI Chat dropdown. Unused models can be deleted from Settings to free disk space.

docs.title: 21. Inventory Management Best Practices
docs.text: Your inventory is the backbone of your preparedness. Here is how to get the most out of it:

docs.title: Setting Up Your Inventory
docs.text:
1. Start with critical categories: Water, Food, Medical, and Batteries/Power. These are what you need in the first 72 hours.
  2. Use specific names: "Canned black beans 15oz" is better than "beans." You will thank yourself later.
  3. Set minimum quantities: This triggers low-stock alerts. For water, a good minimum is 7 gallons per person.
  4. Set daily usage rates: Even rough estimates help. If your family drinks 2 gallons of water per day, enter 2.0. NOMAD will calculate exactly how many days of supply you have left.
  5. Add expiration dates: NOMAD alerts you 14 days before items expire, so you can rotate stock.
  6. Use locations: "Garage shelf 2", "Kitchen pantry", "Bug-out bag." This helps during an actual emergency when seconds count.

docs.title: Daily Use
docs.text:
• Daily Consume button: Click this once per day to automatically subtract daily usage from all tracked items. One click updates everything.
  • Quick +/- buttons: Use the arrow buttons on each row to adjust quantities without opening the edit form.
  • Shopping List: Click "Shopping List" to auto-generate a list of items that are low, expiring, or running out based on burn rates.

docs.title: Recommended Categories
docs.text: Category Example Items Daily Usage Tip Water Stored water jugs, water filters, purification tablets 1 gallon per person per day (minimum) Food Canned goods, rice, beans, freeze-dried meals, MREs 2,000 calories per person per day Medical First aid kits, prescription meds, OTC meds, bandages Track daily medications individually Fuel Gasoline, propane, firewood, charcoal, lamp oil Generator: 0.5-1.5 gal/hour depending on load Batteries AA, AAA, D, 9V, CR123A, rechargeable packs Flashlight: ~1 set per 20 hours of use Hygiene Toilet paper, soap, toothpaste, trash bags, bleach Toilet paper: ~1 roll per person per week Tools Multi-tool, duct tape, rope, tarps, fire starters Usually no daily usage (set to 0) Communications Radios, batteries for radios, antenna cables, solar chargers Radio batteries: depends on usage

docs.title: 22. Printable Reports & Emergency Cards
docs.text:
NOMAD can generate several printable documents. Print these and keep physical copies in your go-bag, vehicle, and at rally points: - Inventory Report — Preparedness > Inventory > click Print. Full supply list with quantities, locations, and expiration dates. - Contact Directory — Preparedness > Contacts > click Print. All emergency contacts with phone numbers, callsigns, rally points, and medical notes. - Patient Care Card — Preparedness > Medical > select a patient > click Print Card. Individual medical info including allergies, medications, conditions, and recent vitals. - Emergency Reference Sheet — Home page > scroll to services > Emergency Sheet button. One-page comprehensive sheet with critical contacts, inventory summary, weather, waypoints, and quick-reference information. - Family Emergency Card — Preparedness > Family Plan > click Print Emergency Card. Wallet-size card with meeting locations, evacuation routes, and emergency contacts. - Decision Guide Procedure Card — Preparedness > Guides > complete a guide > click Print. Shows the decision path you followed with the final recommendation. - Emergency Procedures Wallet Card — Preparedness > Procedures > click Wallet Card. Condensed quick-reference for CPR, bleeding, choking, and other critical procedures.

Important: Digital tools can fail. Always keep printed copies of your most critical information. Laminate cards that go in your bag or vehicle. Update and reprint whenever your information changes significantly.

docs.title: 23. LAN Access & Multi-Device Use
docs.text: NOMAD is accessible from any device on your local network — phones, tablets, laptops — not just the computer it is installed on.

docs.title: How to Access from Other Devices
docs.text:
• Find your LAN address in Settings (shown at the top, looks like http://192.168.1.50:8080)

  • Open a browser on your phone, tablet, or other computer

  • Type that address into the browser and press Enter

Everyone on your network gets the same full interface. Changes made on any device are saved to the central database immediately.

docs.title: LAN Chat
docs.text: The chat bubble in the bottom-left corner is a local network messenger. Anyone accessing NOMAD from any device can send messages to each other — no internet or cell service needed. Useful for coordinating during an emergency when phones are down but your local network (router/Wi-Fi) is still running.

docs.title: Dashboard Password
docs.text: If you want to restrict who can access NOMAD on your network, set a dashboard password in Settings > Dashboard Password. Anyone accessing from outside localhost will need to enter the password.

docs.title: 24. Understanding the 6 Services
docs.text:
NOMAD manages 6 separate tools. Each one is optional — install only what you need:

Service What It Does Size Who Needs It AI Chat Private AI assistant that answers questions offline ~310 MB + models Everyone — this is the core feature Information Library Offline Wikipedia, medical references, survival guides ~60 MB + content packs Everyone — essential reference material CyberChef Data encoding, encryption, decryption, hashing, file analysis ~30 MB Technical users who need encryption/encoding tools Education Platform Khan Academy courses, textbooks, progress tracking ~200 MB + courses Families with children, long-term learning Vector Search AI-powered document search for uploaded files ~80 MB Users who upload many documents for AI analysis PDF Tools Merge, split, compress, convert, OCR PDFs ~100 MB + Java Users who work with PDF documents frequently

Minimal setup: Install just AI Chat + Information Library for a powerful offline knowledge base. Add others as needed.

docs.title: Power Outage (first 24 hours)
docs.text:
1. Open NOMAD on your laptop (it runs on battery)
  2. Check Inventory > filter by "Food" — see what is perishable and eat that first
  3. Use the Guides > Power Outage Response for step-by-step instructions
  4. Log an incident (Preparedness > Incidents) so you have a record
  5. If extended, use the Calculators > Generator Fuel to plan fuel usage
  6. Check the Weather tab — is a storm causing this? Monitor pressure trends

docs.title: Medical Emergency
docs.text:
1. Open Preparedness > Procedures > search for the relevant procedure (CPR, bleeding, burns, etc.)
  2. If treating someone: go to Medical tab, find or add the patient, record vitals
  3. Check Drug Interactions before giving any medications
  4. Ask the AI (set to "Field Medic" mode) for advice specific to the situation
  5. Log the incident for your records

docs.title: Evacuation
docs.text:
1. Pull up your Family Plan — confirm meeting locations and routes with everyone
  2. Check Inventory > filter by location "Bug-out bag" to verify what is packed
  3. Use Maps to review your evacuation routes offline
  4. Print your Contact Directory and Emergency Card (take physical copies)
  5. If you have time: click "Daily Consume" to update your inventory as you grab items

docs.title: Long-Term Off-Grid Living
docs.text:
• Daily: Click "Daily Consume" to track supply burn rates. Log weather observations. Write a journal entry.
  • Weekly: Check the Shopping List for items running low. Review alerts. Run a checklist audit.
  • Monthly: Run a Benchmark to confirm system health. Back up your database to USB. Review and rotate expiring inventory. Update garden records with harvest data.
  • Quarterly: Run a Training Scenario. Update your Family Plan. Review and update checklists. Sync with other NOMAD systems if applicable.

docs.title: 26. Calculators Reference
docs.text:
The Preparedness > Calculators tab contains survival math tools. Here is what each one does:

Calculator Inputs What It Calculates Water Storage People, days, climate Total gallons needed. Hot climate adds 50%. Includes drinking, cooking, and hygiene water. Food Storage People, days, calories/person/day Total calories needed. Compares your answer to LDS (2,000 cal) and FEMA (2,400 cal) standards. Power / Solar Sizing Load watts, hours/day, days, sun hours Total watt-hours, recommended battery capacity (Ah), minimum solar panel wattage. Accounts for 80% depth of discharge and system efficiency. Watch Schedule Team members, shift length, days Full rotation schedule showing who is on watch when. Ensures equal rest periods. Solar Position Latitude, longitude, date Sunrise, sunset, solar noon, day length. Useful for planning activities and solar panel orientation. Moon Phase Date Current moon phase and illumination. A full moon means better visibility at night but also means you are more visible. Coordinate Converter Latitude, longitude Converts between decimal degrees (38.8977) and degrees-minutes-seconds (38°53\'51.7"N). Useful for radio communication and map reading. Travel Time Distance, mode, terrain Estimated travel time by foot, bicycle, horse, or vehicle across flat, hilly, mountain, or swamp terrain. Accounts for reduced speed on difficult ground. Battery Life Capacity (mAh), voltage Watt-hours available and estimated runtime for common devices (flashlight, radio, phone, laptop). Bleach Calculator Bleach %, water clarity, gallons Exact drops or teaspoons of bleach needed to purify water. Adjusts for cloudy water (double dose). Includes surface disinfection ratios. Resource Planner People, days, activity level Comprehensive resource needs: water, food (calories), fuel, medical supplies. Cross-references with your actual inventory to show gaps.

Tip: The Resource Planner is the most powerful calculator — it pulls your real inventory data and shows exactly what you have vs. what you need for a given scenario.

docs.title: 27. NukeMap Guide
docs.text: NukeMap v3.2.0 is a nuclear effects simulator bundled with NOMAD. It runs entirely offline.

docs.title: What It Can Do
docs.text:
• Blast Radius Visualization — Shows concentric rings for fireball, severe damage, moderate damage, and light damage zones for any weapon yield
  • Thermal Radiation — Shows how far burns and fire ignition extend from ground zero
  • Fallout Plume — Models fallout patterns based on wind direction and weapon yield
  • Shelter Analysis — Calculates radiation exposure based on shelter type and distance
  • Multiple Detonation Scenarios — 7 pre-built WW3 scenarios with 708 warheads across 418 real-world targets
  • EMP/HEMP Burst — Visualize electromagnetic pulse effects from high-altitude detonations

docs.title: How to Use It
docs.text:
1. Click Launch NukeMap in the Tools tab
  2. Select a weapon from the sidebar or enter a custom yield
  3. Click anywhere on the map to place the detonation point
  4. Examine the colored rings to understand the blast effects at different distances
  5. Use the scenarios panel to load pre-built conflict simulations

Purpose: NukeMap is an educational and planning tool. Understanding blast effects helps you evaluate whether your location is within risk zones and plan appropriate shelter and evacuation routes. This is not a toy — it is a serious planning tool used by emergency management professionals.

docs.title: 28. Notes & Documentation
docs.text: The Notes tab is your digital notebook for plans, procedures, observations, and logs.

docs.title: Markdown Support
docs.text:
Notes support Markdown formatting. Some examples:

Type This To Get This # Heading Large heading ## Subheading Smaller heading **bold text** bold text *italic text* italic text - item Bullet point 1. item Numbered list [link text](url) Clickable link

docs.title: Organizing Notes
docs.text:
• Tags — Add comma-separated tags (e.g., "medical, procedures, first-aid") to categorize. Use the search to filter by tag.
  • Pinning — Pin critical notes (SOPs, emergency contacts, rally point coordinates) so they always appear at the top.
  • Export — Export a single note as a .md file or all notes as a ZIP archive. Print important notes and keep physical copies.

docs.title: Recommended Notes to Create
docs.text:
• Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) for your household
  • Rally point coordinates and descriptions
  • Neighborhood contact list and skills inventory
  • Water source locations and purification notes
  • Generator maintenance schedule and fuel rotation log
  • Garden planting schedule and crop rotation plan
  • Radio communication protocols and check-in schedules

docs.title: Does NOMAD need the internet to work?
docs.text: No. After you download services, AI models, and content packs, everything works completely offline forever. The only time it connects to the internet is when YOU choose to download something new.

docs.title: Is my data sent to any server or cloud?
docs.text: No. Zero telemetry, zero cloud, zero tracking. All data stays on your computer in a local database file. The AI runs on your hardware — your conversations are never sent anywhere.

docs.title: Can I run NOMAD from a USB drive?
docs.text: Yes. Download the portable NOMADFieldDesk.exe and put it on a USB drive. Run it from there. You can choose to store data on the USB drive as well during the setup wizard.

docs.title: How much disk space do I need?
docs.text: The base application is ~25 MB. With essential content and a small AI model: ~5 GB. With everything downloaded: 50-200 GB depending on how many content packs and AI models you install. You control what you download.

docs.title: Can my family use it from another computer on the network?
docs.text: Yes. As long as the other computer is on the same Wi-Fi or LAN, it can open a browser and go to your NOMAD address shown in Settings. Remote access is intended for desktop-class browsers on your local network.

docs.title: What happens if my computer breaks?
docs.text: If you have been backing up (Settings > Full Backup), you can restore your entire database on another computer. Keep backup ZIPs on a USB drive in a separate location. The portable exe runs on any Windows 10/11 PC with no installation.

docs.title: Can I share my checklists with friends who also use NOMAD?
docs.text: Yes. Go to Preparedness > Checklists, click a checklist, then click Export JSON. Send the file to your friend and they can click Import to add it to their system. You can also use the sync features to share all data at once.

docs.title: How do I update NOMAD?
docs.text: Download the latest version from the releases page. If using the portable exe, just replace the old file with the new one. If using the installer, run the new installer — it updates in place. Your data is stored separately and is preserved across updates.

docs.title: Why do some features say "Install" before I can use them?
docs.text: NOMAD manages several separate tools (AI Chat, Information Library, etc.). Each needs to be downloaded once before use. This keeps the base application small and lets you choose what you need. Most features in the Preparedness tab work immediately with no installation required.

docs.title: The AI gives wrong or unhelpful answers. What should I do?
docs.text: Try a larger AI model (see the AI Model guide above). Smaller models are fast but less accurate. Also try changing the conversation mode — "Medical Advisor" gives better medical answers than "General Assistant." Enable "Include My Prep Data" so the AI has context about your specific situation.

docs.title: 30. Medical Module In Depth
docs.text: The Medical tab in Preparedness is a full field-hospital record system. Here is how to use it effectively:

docs.title: Setting Up Patients
docs.text:
1. Go to Preparedness > Medical and click Add Patient
  2. If the person is already in your Contacts, click Import from Contacts instead — it copies their name, blood type, and medical notes automatically
  3. Fill in: age, weight (kg), sex, blood type, allergies, current medications, and existing conditions
  4. Use JSON array format for allergies/medications/conditions: ["Penicillin", "Latex"]

docs.title: Recording Vitals
docs.text:
Click on a patient, then click Add Vitals. Enter any or all of:

Vital Normal Range (Adult) Color Coding Blood Pressure 90-140 / 60-90 Red if systolic >180 or <80 Pulse 60-100 bpm Red if >120 or <50 Respiration 12-20 breaths/min Red if >30 or <8 Temperature 97.0-99.5 °F Red if >103 or <95 SpO2 95-100% Red if <90%, orange if <94% Pain Level 0 (none) to 10 (worst) Red if 8+, orange if 5-7 GCS (Glasgow Coma Scale) 15 (alert) to 3 (unresponsive) Red if <9

Tip: Record vitals every 15-30 minutes during an active medical situation. The trend over time is often more important than any single reading. NOMAD tracks the history so you can see if a patient is improving or declining.

docs.title: Drug Interaction Checker
docs.text:
Before giving medications, check for dangerous interactions. The checker covers 26 common interaction pairs including: - NSAIDs (ibuprofen, aspirin, naproxen) with blood thinners - Acetaminophen (Tylenol) with alcohol or liver medications - SSRIs with other serotonergic drugs (serotonin syndrome risk) - Opioids with benzodiazepines (respiratory depression risk) - ACE inhibitors with potassium supplements (hyperkalemia risk)

Warning: The drug interaction checker is a reference tool, not a replacement for professional medical advice. In a true emergency, use your best judgment and seek professional care as soon as possible.

docs.title: Wound Documentation
docs.text: The wound log supports 8 wound types (laceration, puncture, abrasion, burn, fracture, crush, bite, other) and 4 severity levels (minor, moderate, severe, critical). Record the body location, description, and treatment given. This creates a timeline that is invaluable if the patient later reaches professional medical care.

docs.title: 31. Food Production Guide
docs.text: The Garden tab helps you track everything related to growing and raising your own food.

docs.title: Garden Plots
docs.text: Add each growing area with its dimensions (width x length in feet), sun exposure (full sun, partial shade, full shade), and soil type. NOMAD calculates total square footage automatically. This helps you plan how much you can grow.

docs.title: Seed Inventory & Viability
docs.text:
Seeds lose viability over time. NOMAD tracks 25 species with species-specific shelf life data:

Species Years Viable Notes Onion, Parsnip, Parsley 1-2 years Use quickly; short shelf life Corn, Pepper, Beans 2-3 years Moderate shelf life Tomato, Squash, Carrot 3-5 years Good keepers if stored dry and cool Cucumber, Lettuce, Radish 5+ years Longest viable; excellent for stockpiling

Enter the year each seed was harvested and NOMAD calculates current viability. If seeds show less than 50% viability, plant extra to compensate for lower germination rates.

docs.title: Harvest Logging
docs.text: When you harvest produce, log it in the Harvest tab. Specify the crop, quantity, unit (lbs, heads, bunches), and which plot it came from. Harvests automatically create or update items in your Inventory — so your supply counts stay accurate without duplicate entry.

docs.title: Livestock
docs.text: Track animals by species (chicken, goat, cattle, pig, rabbit, sheep, duck, turkey, horse, bee), with individual names or tag numbers, date of birth, sex, weight, and status (active, sold, deceased). Log health events (vaccinations, illnesses, treatments) on each animal with timestamps.

docs.title: USDA Hardiness Zone
docs.text: Enter your latitude and NOMAD estimates your USDA plant hardiness zone (3a through 11a+). This tells you which plants can survive your winters and when to plant. The lookup works offline using latitude-based approximation.

docs.title: 32. Power Management Guide
docs.text: The Power tab helps you track your energy infrastructure and project how long you can sustain operations.

docs.title: Device Registry
docs.text:
Register every power device you own. Each type has specific fields:

Device Type Key Specs to Record Solar Panel Wattage, voltage, type (monocrystalline, polycrystalline, thin-film) Battery Capacity (Ah), voltage, type (lead-acid, LiFePO4, lithium-ion, AGM) Charge Controller Amperage, type (MPPT, PWM), max voltage Inverter Wattage (continuous and peak), input voltage, output (120V/240V) Generator Wattage, fuel type, fuel capacity, runtime per tank

docs.title: Power Logging
docs.text: Log daily readings: battery voltage, state of charge (SOC%), solar watts produced, solar watt-hours today, load watts consumed, load watt-hours today, and whether the generator is running. Over time, this builds a picture of your energy balance.

docs.title: Autonomy Dashboard
docs.text: The dashboard calculates your net daily energy balance (solar production minus consumption) and projects how many days your batteries will last: - Green gauge — More than 7 days of autonomy. You are energy-sustainable. - Orange gauge — 3-7 days. Start reducing consumption or increasing production. - Red gauge — Less than 3 days. Critical — activate generator or drastically cut loads.

docs.title: Camera Setup
docs.text:
NOMAD can display live feeds from IP cameras on your network. Three stream types are supported: - MJPEG — Continuous video stream. Most common for budget cameras. Example URL: http://192.168.1.100/cgi-bin/mjpg/video.cgi - Snapshot — Still image that auto-refreshes every 5 seconds. Lower bandwidth. Example: http://192.168.1.100/snap.cgi - HLS — High-quality video stream. Used by newer cameras. Example: http://192.168.1.100/live/stream.m3u8

Common camera brands and typical URLs:

Brand Typical MJPEG URL Reolink http://IP/cgi-bin/api.cgi?cmd=Snap Amcrest http://IP/cgi-bin/mjpg/video.cgi?channel=1 Wyze (with firmware) rtsp://IP/live (requires bridge) Generic ONVIF http://IP/onvif-http/snapshot?Profile_1

docs.title: Access Logging
docs.text: The access log tracks who comes and goes from your location. Log entries include: person name, direction (entry, exit, or patrol), location (front gate, back door, perimeter), method (visual, camera, sensor, radio report), and notes. Use this during heightened security situations to maintain awareness of all movements.

docs.title: Security Dashboard
docs.text: The dashboard aggregates: current threat level (from your situation board), number of active cameras, access events in the last 24 hours, and incidents in the last 48 hours. This gives you a single-glance security overview.

docs.title: 34. Weather Tracking Guide
docs.text: The Weather tab lets you record manual weather observations. This is crucial when you are offline and cannot check weather forecasts.

docs.title: What to Record
docs.text:
• Barometric Pressure (hPa) — The most important reading. Falling pressure predicts storms. Rising pressure indicates clearing weather. A drop of more than 4 hPa in a few hours is a strong storm warning. Requires a barometer — many outdoor thermometers include one.
  • Temperature (°F) — Current reading. Track trends to anticipate heating/cooling needs.
  • Wind Direction & Speed — Note where wind is coming from (N, NE, E, etc.) and estimated speed. Shifting winds often precede weather changes.
  • Cloud Cover — Clear, partly cloudy, overcast, threatening. Cloud types can indicate approaching weather.
  • Precipitation — Rain, snow, sleet, hail, fog, or none. Note intensity (light, moderate, heavy).
  • Visibility — Good, reduced, poor. Important for travel decisions.

docs.title: Reading the Trends
docs.text:
NOMAD automatically analyzes your pressure readings over time: - Steady (change < 1 hPa) — Current weather likely to continue - Slowly falling (1-3 hPa) — Weather may deteriorate in 12-24 hours - Rapidly falling (> 4 hPa) — Storm approaching. NOMAD generates an automatic alert. - Rising — Weather improving. If rising rapidly after a storm, clearing is imminent.

Tip: Record weather at the same times each day (e.g., morning and evening) for the most useful trend data. Even without instruments, noting cloud patterns and wind changes has value.

docs.title: 35. Communications & Radio Guide
docs.text: NOMAD includes several tools for emergency communications:

docs.title: Radio Reference Table
docs.text:
Preparedness > Radio has a complete frequency reference:

Service Channels Range License Needed FRS (Family Radio Service) 22 0.5-2 miles No GMRS (General Mobile Radio) 30 1-25 miles Yes ($35, covers family) MURS (Multi-Use Radio) 5 1-5 miles No CB (Citizens Band) 40 2-15 miles No HAM (Amateur Radio) Many Local to worldwide Yes (exam required)

docs.title: Key Emergency Frequencies
docs.text:
• FRS Channel 1 — Common rally/meeting channel
  • FRS Channel 3 — Widely used emergency channel
  • GMRS Channel 20 — Emergency/travel channel
  • HAM 146.520 MHz — 2-meter calling frequency (most popular)
  • HAM 446.000 MHz — 70cm calling frequency
  • NOAA Weather — 162.400-162.550 MHz (7 channels, listen-only)
  • CB Channel 9 — Official emergency channel
  • CB Channel 19 — Highway/trucker channel (road conditions)

docs.title: LAN Chat
docs.text: The chat bubble in the bottom-left corner lets anyone on your local network send messages to each other. This works when cell phones are down but your Wi-Fi router is still running (even on generator or battery power). Set your name in the chat window so others know who is messaging.

docs.title: Emergency Broadcast
docs.text: In the Command Post tab, the Emergency Broadcast feature sends a high-priority message to every device currently connected to NOMAD. It appears as a banner at the top of the screen with an alert sound. Use this for urgent group notifications like “Evacuate now” or “Shelter in place.”

docs.title: 36. Secure Vault Guide
docs.text: The Secure Vault encrypts sensitive information with AES-256-GCM — the same encryption standard used by banks and the military.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Set a master password the first time you open the Vault
  2. Enter this password each time you want to view or add entries
  3. All data is encrypted in your browser before being stored — even NOMAD itself cannot read your vault without the password
  4. If you forget your password, there is no recovery — the encryption is real and cannot be bypassed

docs.title: What to Store
docs.text:
• Account passwords and PINs
  • Safe combinations and lock codes
  • GPS coordinates of caches, rally points, or sensitive locations
  • Insurance policy numbers and account details
  • Legal document references (will locations, power of attorney details)
  • Medical record numbers
  • Cryptocurrency wallet seeds or recovery phrases

Critical: Write your vault password on paper and store it in a physically secure location (fireproof safe, safety deposit box). If you lose the password, your vault entries are permanently unrecoverable.

docs.title: Password Generator
docs.text: The built-in password generator creates strong random passwords. Adjust the length and click Generate. Use the show/hide toggle to verify the password before saving it.

docs.title: 37. Training Scenarios In Depth
docs.text: The 4 training scenarios are designed to test your decision-making under pressure. Here is what to expect:

docs.title: Grid Down — 7 Days
docs.text: The longest and most comprehensive scenario. You will face decisions about: - Phase 1: Initial power loss — immediate actions, device protection - Phase 2: Water supply — municipal water may fail, alternative sources - Phase 3: Food management — refrigeration lost, prioritize perishables - Phase 4: Security — darkness increases vulnerability, neighbor coordination - Phase 5: Medical — managing without electric medical devices - Phase 6: Communications — establishing contact with outside world - Phase 7: Recovery — power restored, damage assessment, after-action review

docs.title: How AI Complications Work
docs.text:
Between phases, the AI has a 50% chance of injecting a complication based on your actual data. For example: - If your water inventory is low, it might say: “Your water supply has dropped to 4 gallons. A neighbor asks to share.” - If you have a patient registered, it might say: “[Patient name] is showing signs of dehydration.” - If you have no generator in your power devices, it might say: “The family next door has a generator and offers to let you charge devices — but they want something in trade.”

This makes every run unique and realistic. The more data you put into NOMAD, the more personalized the scenarios become.

docs.title: Scoring
docs.text:
After completing all phases, the AI grades your performance 0-100 based on: - Did you address immediate threats first? - Did you conserve resources appropriately? - Did you consider the needs of all household members? - Did you maintain communications and security? - Were your decisions consistent and logical?

Scores are saved to your history so you can track improvement over time. Aim for 80+ on all four scenarios.

docs.title: 38. Task Scheduler
docs.text:
The Task Scheduler (Settings tab) lets you create recurring tasks for maintenance, medical, patrol, garden, and inventory activities.

Feature Description Recurring Tasks Set tasks to repeat daily, weekly, or monthly. When completed, the next due date auto-calculates. Categories Maintenance, Medical, Patrol, Garden, Inventory, Custom — color-coded in the task list. Assignment Assign tasks to team members from your Contacts list. Overdue Alerts Overdue tasks appear as predictive alerts in the alert bar with a "PREDICTED" badge. Sunrise/Sunset The dashboard sun widget shows sunrise, sunset, and day length for solar panel and patrol planning.

Examples: "Check water filter" (weekly), "Generator oil change" (monthly), "Medication dose" (daily), "Patrol north fence" (daily).

docs.title: 39. AI Memory System
docs.text:
AI Memory lets you store persistent facts that the AI remembers across all conversations and copilot queries.

Feature Description Memory Panel Click the "Memory" button in the AI Chat header to view, add, or delete facts. Persistent Context Facts are injected into every AI interaction — chat, copilot quick-query, and SITREP generation. What to Store Location, group size, medical conditions, equipment specs, key concerns, ongoing situations, preferences. Emergency Sheet AI memory facts appear on the printable Emergency Reference Sheet under "Operator Notes."

Examples: "Family of 4, two children ages 5 and 8", "Solar array is 5kW with 10kWh battery", "Well water requires UV treatment", "Bug-out location is the cabin at waypoint 'Safe House'".

docs.title: 40. Printable Field Documents
docs.text:
NOMAD generates 9 printable documents for when screens aren't available. Access from the Home tab "Printable Field Documents" section or Settings tab.

Document Contents Best For Operations Binder Complete reference: TOC, contacts, frequencies, medical cards, inventory, checklists, waypoints, procedures, family plan Keep in go-bag or command post. Replace monthly. Wallet Cards 5 credit-card-sized cards: ICE, blood type, medications, rally points, frequencies Laminate and carry on person at all times. SOI Signal Operating Instructions: frequency assignments, call sign matrix, radio profiles, net schedule Issue to all radio operators. Destroy when outdated. Emergency Sheet One-page critical data aggregate with contacts, supplies, patients, waypoints, weather, tasks, AI notes Post on wall at base camp. Update weekly. Medical Cards Per-patient vital signs, medications, conditions Give to each patient's caregiver. Bug-Out List Grab-and-go checklist with rally points Post near exit doors. Inventory Report Full supply list with quantities, locations, expiration dates Physical inventory verification. Contact Directory Complete personnel directory with all details Keep copy at each rally point. Frequency Card Standard emergency frequencies + team contacts Laminate pocket-sized for each radio operator.

docs.title: 41. Glossary
docs.text:
Term Definition AI Model A downloadable file that gives the AI its knowledge and reasoning ability. Larger models = better answers but more storage and RAM required. BOB (Bug-Out Bag) A pre-packed bag with essentials for a 72-hour evacuation. Also called a go-bag, GOOD bag, or INCH bag. Burn Rate How fast you are consuming a supply. Measured in daily usage. NOMAD uses this to calculate "Days Left." CBRN Chemical, Biological, Radiological, Nuclear. A category of threats requiring specialized protective measures. Content Pack A downloadable file containing offline reference material (Wikipedia articles, medical databases, survival guides, etc.). GCS (Glasgow Coma Scale) A 3-15 score measuring consciousness. 15 = fully alert, <9 = severe impairment, 3 = unresponsive. GPX GPS Exchange Format. A standard file format for sharing waypoints and tracks between GPS devices and mapping software. hPa (hectopascal) Unit of barometric pressure. Standard sea-level pressure is 1013 hPa. Falling pressure indicates approaching storms. LAN Local Area Network. Your home Wi-Fi network. NOMAD is accessible from any device on your LAN. Meshtastic An open-source project for long-range, low-power mesh networking using inexpensive radio hardware. No cell towers needed. Node A single NOMAD installation. When syncing between multiple computers, each is called a "node." OPSEC Operations Security. Practices to prevent adversaries from learning about your plans, capabilities, and vulnerabilities. Rally Point A pre-designated meeting location for your family or group during an emergency. SITREP Situation Report. A standardized format for summarizing the current state of an emergency or operation. SOC (State of Charge) How full a battery is, expressed as a percentage (0-100%). Similar to your phone battery percentage. SpO2 Blood oxygen saturation level. Normal is 95-100%. Below 90% is a medical emergency. START Triage Simple Triage and Rapid Treatment. A system for prioritizing multiple casualties: walk → breathe → pulse → mental status. Waypoint A saved location on the map with coordinates, name, and optional notes. NOMAD Field Desk v [[Official site](https://www.projectnomad.us) — GitHub](https://github.com/SysAdminDoc/project-nomad-desktop) — Discord Community

Built on the original Project N.O.M.A.D. foundation by Crosstalk Solutions. Desktop edition and ongoing expansion by SysAdminDoc. Knowledge that never goes offline.
